regulator: FSA (Financial Services Agency) / Bank of Japan (BOJ); Ministry of Finance (MOF), which convenes the government-BOJ CBDC liaison conference
instruments: Banking Act (銀行法, Act No. 59 of 1981), Art. 14-2 -- the general, technology-neutral statutory basis for capital-adequacy-ratio regulation ('銀行の保有する資産等に照らし当該銀行の自己資本の充実の状況が適当であるかどうかの基準'), delegating the actual standard to Prime Minister-level notices; confirmed by direct fetch of the Act's full consolidated text via the e-Gov Law API (law_id 356AC0000000059), which contains no occurrence of '暗号資産' (crypto asset) anywhere in the Act itself -- crypto-specific capital treatment lives at sub-statutory notice/Q&A level, not in the Banking Act text, consistent with this jurisdiction's amend-the-existing-framework pattern

FSA, '自己資本比率規制に関するQ&A等の公表（更新）について' (Publication of Updated Q&A on Capital Adequacy Ratio Regulations), 19 October 2022 -- clarifies prudential (capital-adequacy) treatment for trust banks' self-holding of crypto assets, stating verbatim that '信託銀行が暗号資産を自己保有するニーズが高まる可能性があることから、健全性規制上の取扱いを明確化しました' (given a possible rise in trust banks' need to self-hold crypto assets, prudential treatment has been clarified), and that the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision was examining international cryptoasset prudential treatment ('バーゼル銀行監督委員会において、暗号資産の健全性規制上の取扱いについて検討が行われている'), with Japan's own treatment to be reconsidered once international agreement is reached; confirmed by direct fetch, https://www.fsa.go.jp/news/r4/ginkou/20221019-2/20221019.html -- this notice, not a dedicated Basel-implementing notice, is the primary source located this session for Japan's crypto-related prudential treatment; FSA's own Basel-regulation index page (basel_ii/index.html), also fetched directly, lists this Q&A under a '暗号資産' heading but adds no further implementation detail

Banks' restriction to holding crypto assets only to the minimum necessary extent, and prohibition on banks conducting crypto-asset exchange business directly through the bank entity itself (commonly described in industry/legal commentary as a 'Bank Holding Rule') -- corroborated only through secondary legal-commentary sources this session (a March 2026 industry policy brief), not through independent fetch of a specific FSA supervisory-guideline article stating it in those terms; understood to rest on the Banking Act's general business-scope restriction on banks (他業禁止, Art. 10-12) read together with FSA supervisory guidance, but the exact guideline citation was not independently confirmed this session -- see open_items

Financial System Council, Working Group on the Crypto-Asset System (金融審議会「暗号資産制度に関するワーキング・グループ」) -- met six times between 31 July 2025 and 26 November 2025 and published its report on 10 December 2025 (confirmed by direct fetch of the WG's index page, https://www.fsa.go.jp/singi/singi_kinyu/angoshisanseido_wg/angoshisanseido_wg_index.html); the report itself (https://www.fsa.go.jp/singi/singi_kinyu/tosin/20251210/01.pdf, a 48-page Japanese-language PDF confirmed to exist and be FSA-authored via its document metadata) could not be machine-extracted as readable text this session, so this pillar does not assert any specific WG finding on bank crypto-holding rules or prudential treatment from the report itself -- see open_items; the WG's separate April 2025 discussion paper ('暗号資産に関連する制度のあり方等の検証', confirmed by direct fetch of its announcement page, https://www.fsa.go.jp/news/r6/sonota/20250410_2/crypto_dp.html) is tracked in the Funds & ETFs pillar for its ETF-eligibility framing and cross-referenced rather than duplicated here

FSA 'FinTech実証実験ハブ・決済高度化プロジェクト（PIP: Payment Innovation Project）' -- the PIP was launched within FSA's existing FinTech Proof-of-Concept Hub (established 21 September 2017) on 7 November 2025; on 3 April 2026 FSA announced its third PIP-supported pilot, an interbank settlement experiment for tokenized bank deposits between customers of different banks, testing both an inter-bank deposit-account method and a stablecoin-based method, with the stated purpose of verifying practical utility/feasibility and organising related legal issues ('実務的な有用性や実現可能性を検証し、関連する法的論点の整理を行い'), including compliance, supervisory, and consumer-facing legal-interpretation issues; confirmed by direct fetch, https://www.fsa.go.jp/news/r7/sonota/20260403.html -- this pillar describes the pilot's regulatory purpose only, consistent with the named-entity rule, without naming the participating firms

Bank of Japan, 'The Bank of Japan's Approach to Central Bank Digital Currency' (9 October 2020) -- the Bank's foundational published CBDC policy statement, still linked from its current CBDC portal, stating verbatim: 'the Bank of Japan currently has no plan to issue CBDC' while committing to 'prepare thoroughly to respond to changes in circumstances in an appropriate manner,' and that whether to introduce CBDC ultimately depends on gaining sufficient public understanding; confirmed by direct fetch, https://www.boj.or.jp/en/about/release_2020/rel201009e.htm

Bank of Japan, Central Bank Digital Currency Experiments: Pilot Program -- commenced 17 February 2023 per BOJ's own commencement announcement, following Proof of Concept Phase 1 (completed May 2022) and Phase 2 (completed May 2023); the Pilot Program's aims, per BOJ's own CBDC portal, are to test technical feasibility not fully covered by the PoCs and to draw on private-sector technical/operational expertise for CBDC-ecosystem design in the event of eventual social implementation; confirmed by direct fetch, https://www.boj.or.jp/en/paym/digital/index.htm

Bank of Japan, CBDC Forum (CBDCフォーラム) -- established with its first plenary meeting on 20 July 2023 as the private-sector engagement body supporting the Pilot Program; held its sixth plenary meeting on 4 June 2026, with the meeting summary published 30 June 2026; confirmed by direct fetch of the Forum's own page, https://www.boj.or.jp/paym/digital/d_forum/index.htm -- this pillar does not independently verify secondary reporting that BOJ's June 2026 progress report describes a reorganisation of the Forum's seven working groups into three discussion groups, since the underlying BOJ PDF (dig260610a.pdf) could not be machine-extracted this session -- see open_items

Bank of Japan, Liaison and Coordination Committee on Central Bank Digital Currency -- BOJ's own private-sector/expert liaison body (distinct from the government-agency liaison conference below), holding its first meeting 26 March 2021 (per Executive Director Uchida's opening remarks, https://www.boj.or.jp/en/about/press/koen_2021/ko210326a.htm) and, per BOJ's CBDC portal, its eleventh meeting with remarks dated 29 May 2026

Ministry of Finance / Bank of Japan, 'CBDC（中央銀行デジタル通貨）に関する関係府省庁・日本銀行連絡会議' (Liaison Conference of Relevant Government Ministries/Agencies and the Bank of Japan on CBDC) -- a separate, government-side coordination body established pursuant to the Cabinet-decided 'Basic Policy on Economic and Fiscal Management and Reform 2023' (骨太の方針2023, decided 16 June 2023) to organise the basic institutional-design framework as between the government and the Bank of Japan; its most recently listed session as of this research is a written-form 'progress report' session dated 19 June 2026 (令和８年６月１９日); confirmed by direct fetch of MOF's own conference page, https://www.mof.go.jp/about_mof/councils/meeting_of_cbdcre/index.html -- the page's linked '根拠・構成員' (legal basis / membership) document, listing the conference's exact member ministries/agencies, was not independently machine-extracted this session -- see open_items

Bank of Japan Governor Ueda, opening remarks/speech at FIN/SUM 2026, '新金融エコシステムにおける中央銀行の役割' (The Role of the Central Bank in the New Financial Ecosystem), 3 March 2026 -- states BOJ is 'studying' ('検討しています') mechanisms to issue central bank money as blockchain-based 'tokenized deposits' with smart-contract capability, is internally running a sandbox project to technically verify blockchain-based settlement of Bank of Japan current-account deposits ('進めています' / '検証していきます'), and participates alongside multiple overseas central banks and major private financial institutions in 'Project Agorá', an international cross-border-settlement experiment; the speech frames all of this as exploratory, with no decision announced; confirmed by direct fetch, https://www.boj.or.jp/about/press/koen_2026/ko260303a.htm
standing_summary: Japan has no dedicated statute for banking- or central-bank-money-related crypto activity: this pillar covers how the FSA applies the existing, technology-neutral Banking Act's capital-adequacy framework to banks' and trust banks' crypto exposure, how tokenized bank deposits are being tested through an FSA-supervised pilot programme rather than regulated by new legislation, and how the Bank of Japan (BOJ), together with the government via the Ministry of Finance (MOF), is exploring central bank digital currency (CBDC) and tokenized central-bank money -- all without a governing statute specific to this pillar, the same amend-and-apply, sometimes non-statutory-at-all, pattern that runs through this jurisdiction's other pillars. Cross-reference, not duplication: banks, registered fund-transfer service providers, and trust companies are the anchor permitted issuers of digital-money-type stablecoins (electronic payment instruments, 電子決済手段) under the Payment Services Act framework described in full in the Stablecoins pillar; this pillar does not restate that issuer-eligibility analysis. Prudential treatment of banks' crypto exposure: the Banking Act (銀行法, Act No. 59 of 1981) contains no crypto-specific provision at all -- confirmed by direct fetch of the Act's full consolidated text via the e-Gov Law API, which returns zero occurrences of '暗号資産' -- so crypto-related capital treatment is instead addressed at the sub-statutory notice/Q&A level under the Art. 14-2 capital-adequacy-standard delegation. The one primary source located this session on point is FSA's 19 October 2022 update to its capital-adequacy-ratio Q&A, which clarifies prudential treatment for trust banks' self-holding of crypto assets (motivated, per the notice's own wording, by rising demand from trust banks to self-hold crypto) and states that the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision was at that time still examining the international prudential treatment of crypto-assets, with Japan's own treatment to be reconsidered once Basel reaches agreement -- this pillar found no more recent FSA primary notice confirming whether, as of this research, Japan has since formally implemented the Basel Committee's cryptoasset capital standard, only secondary reporting (not independently verified this session) that Japan had not committed to a firm implementation timeline, citing limited domestic bank exposure to crypto-assets as a stated reason; this should be treated as an open question rather than a settled fact pending direct verification of FSA's current position. Separately, industry/legal commentary (not independently primary-verified this session) describes a 'Bank Holding Rule' under which banking groups' crypto-asset holdings must be kept to the minimum necessary and banks are barred from operating crypto-asset exchange business directly through the bank entity itself, while trust banks may hold and provide trust-custody services involving crypto assets as trustee; this pillar flags, rather than asserts, the precise FSA supervisory-guideline citation for that rule pending direct confirmation. The Financial System Council's Working Group on the Crypto-Asset System met six times between 31 July and 26 November 2025 and published a report on 10 December 2025 (confirmed to exist and be FSA-authored via direct fetch of its 48-page PDF's metadata), but this pillar could not machine-extract the report's text this session and therefore does not assert any specific finding from it regarding bank crypto-holding rules, prudential treatment, or the pending FIEA migration bill's implications for banks -- an open item for a future pass. Tokenized deposits: rather than being regulated through new legislation, tokenized bank deposits are being tested through FSA's own supervised pilot infrastructure. FSA's FinTech Proof-of-Concept Hub (established 2017) launched a dedicated 'Payment Innovation Project' (PIP) on 7 November 2025, and on 3 April 2026 FSA announced the PIP's third supported pilot: an interbank settlement experiment for tokenized bank deposits transferred between customers of different banks, testing both an inter-bank-deposit-account settlement method and a stablecoin-based method, with FSA's own stated purpose being to verify practical utility and feasibility and to organise the related legal issues -- including compliance, supervisory, and consumer-facing legal-interpretation questions -- that arise; this pillar describes only the regulatory pilot's scope and purpose, per the named-entity rule, without naming the participating financial institutions. Bank of Japan Governor Ueda's 3 March 2026 remarks at FIN/SUM 2026 place this in a wider BOJ context: the Bank states it is studying mechanisms to issue central bank money itself as blockchain-based 'tokenized deposits' with smart-contract functionality, is running an internal sandbox project to technically verify blockchain-based settlement of BOJ current-account deposits, and participates alongside multiple overseas central banks and major private financial institutions in 'Project Agorá', an international cross-border wholesale-settlement experiment -- all framed by the Governor as exploratory ('under study' / 'will verify'), with no decision announced. Bank of Japan -- retail CBDC (digital yen): BOJ's foundational published position, in 'The Bank of Japan's Approach to Central Bank Digital Currency' (9 October 2020), states plainly that 'the Bank of Japan currently has no plan to issue CBDC,' while committing to prepare thoroughly for changes in circumstances, and frames the ultimate decision as depending on gaining sufficient public understanding -- language still linked as BOJ's foundational CBDC policy statement from its current CBDC portal. Consistent with that position, BOJ commenced a CBDC 'Pilot Program' on 17 February 2023, following two completed Proof-of-Concept phases (through May 2022 and May 2023 respectively), aimed at testing technical feasibility not covered by the PoCs and incorporating private-sector expertise into CBDC-ecosystem design in the event of eventual social implementation -- BOJ's own materials do not describe this as a commitment to issue. The Pilot Program's private-sector engagement body, the CBDC Forum, held its first plenary meeting on 20 July 2023 and its sixth plenary meeting on 4 June 2026 (summary published 30 June 2026), confirmed by direct fetch of the Forum's own page; this pillar does not adopt unverified secondary reporting of a working-group reorganisation described in a BOJ PDF that could not be machine-extracted this session. Two distinct liaison structures track this work at different levels: BOJ's own 'Liaison and Coordination Committee on Central Bank Digital Currency', a private-sector/expert engagement body first convened 26 March 2021 and, per BOJ's portal, now at its eleventh meeting (remarks dated 29 May 2026); and a separate government-side body, the MOF/BOJ 'Liaison Conference of Relevant Government Ministries/Agencies and the Bank of Japan on CBDC', established pursuant to the Cabinet-decided 'Basic Policy on Economic and Fiscal Management and Reform 2023' (16 June 2023) to organise the basic institutional-design framework between the government and BOJ, with its most recent listed session a written-form progress-report session dated 19 June 2026, confirmed by direct fetch of MOF's own conference page -- this pillar did not independently confirm the conference's exact member ministries/agencies (a linked 'legal basis / membership' document was not machine-extracted this session) or the substantive content of its interim reports (April 2024 and May 2025), which could not be machine-extracted either; both are open items for a future pass rather than asserted facts. No decision to issue a digital yen has been made as of this research, consistent with BOJ's own stated position; this pillar makes no prediction about the outcome or timing of any future decision.

key_links.label: e-Gov Law API: Banking Act (銀行法, Act No. 59 of 1981) -- full text, JSON (Art. 14-2 confirmed live this session; no occurrence of 暗号資産 in the Act)
FSA: 自己資本比率規制に関するQ&A等の公表（更新）について (19 October 2022) -- prudential treatment of trust banks' self-holding of crypto assets; Basel Committee coordination noted
FSA: 自己資本比率規制等（バーゼル規制）について (Basel capital-ratio regulation overview/index page, lists the above Q&A under a 暗号資産 heading)
FSA: 金融審議会「暗号資産制度に関するワーキング・グループ」 (Financial System Council Working Group on the Crypto-Asset System) -- index page listing all six 2025 meetings and the 10 December 2025 report
FSA: 金融審議会「暗号資産制度に関するワーキング・グループ」報告 (report, 10 December 2025, PDF -- exists and is FSA-authored per metadata; text not machine-extracted this session)
FSA: 「暗号資産に関連する制度のあり方等の検証」ディスカッション・ペーパーの公表について (10 April 2025 discussion paper announcement; full ETF/market-structure treatment in the Funds & ETFs pillar)
FSA: 「FinTech実証実験ハブ・決済高度化プロジェクト（PIP）」支援決定案件について (3 April 2026) -- tokenized bank-deposit interbank-settlement pilot
Bank of Japan: The Bank of Japan's Approach to Central Bank Digital Currency (9 October 2020) -- foundational 'no plan to issue CBDC' policy statement
Bank of Japan: Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC portal) -- Pilot Program commencement (17 February 2023) and current CBDC materials
Bank of Japan: CBDCフォーラム (CBDC Forum page) -- plenary meeting history through the sixth plenary, 4 June 2026
Bank of Japan: Opening Remarks by Executive Director Uchida at the First Meeting of the Liaison and Coordination Committee on Central Bank Digital Currency (26 March 2021)
Ministry of Finance: CBDC（中央銀行デジタル通貨）に関する関係府省庁・日本銀行連絡会議 (government-BOJ CBDC liaison conference; established per the 16 June 2023 Cabinet decision; most recent session 19 June 2026)
Bank of Japan: Governor Ueda, 新金融エコシステムにおける中央銀行の役割 (FIN/SUM 2026 remarks, 3 March 2026) -- tokenized central-bank money, internal sandbox, Project Agorá

open_items: This research could not independently confirm, via a primary FSA source fetched this session, whether Japan has since implemented the Basel Committee's cryptoasset prudential (capital-treatment) standard for banks. The only primary source located is FSA's 19 October 2022 Q&A update, which states Japan's domestic treatment would be reconsidered once the Basel Committee reached international agreement; secondary reporting (a paywalled Central Banking article, not independently corroborated against a primary FSA notice this session) suggests Japan had not committed to a firm implementation timeline as of that reporting, citing limited domestic bank crypto exposure. This should be re-verified against FSA's current Basel-regulation notices before being treated as Japan's current position.

The 'Bank Holding Rule' (banking groups' crypto-asset holdings limited to the minimum necessary; banks barred from operating crypto-asset exchange business directly, with an exception for trust banks acting as trustee) is sourced in this card only from a March 2026 industry policy brief (secondary commentary), not from independent fetch of the specific FSA supervisory-guideline provision. The exact citation (Comprehensive Guidelines for Supervision of banks, or a specific notice) should be located and fetched directly before this rule is treated as primary-verified.

The Financial System Council's Working Group on the Crypto-Asset System published a 48-page report on 10 December 2025, confirmed to exist and be FSA-authored via direct fetch of its PDF metadata, but its text could not be machine-extracted this session (the same class of PDF-extraction limitation encountered for the FINMA Guidance 06/2024 PDF in this project's Switzerland pillar and the FSA 別紙２ PDF in this jurisdiction's Stablecoins pillar). This card therefore makes no claim about the report's specific findings on bank crypto-asset holding rules, prudential treatment, or any recommendation touching banks; it should be re-fetched with a working PDF-text-extraction path before any such claim is added.

The Bank of Japan's own June 2026 CBDC Pilot Program progress report (dig260610a.pdf, dated 10 June 2026, referenced in Japanese-language search results as covering '84 CBDCフォーラム会合' and a reorganisation of seven working groups into three discussion groups) could not be machine-extracted this session; this card relies only on the CBDC Forum's own webpage (directly fetched, confirming a sixth plenary meeting on 4 June 2026 and a summary published 30 June 2026) and does not assert the working-group reorganisation detail, which was seen only in a search-engine-indexed excerpt, not independently verified against the primary PDF.

The government-BOJ CBDC liaison conference's exact member ministries/agencies (linked from MOF's conference page as a '根拠・構成員' document) and the substantive content of its interim reports -- the first interim summary (17 April 2024) and second interim summary (approximately 22 May 2025) -- were not machine-extracted this session; both PDFs returned compressed binary content rather than readable text. The conference's existence, establishing Cabinet decision (16 June 2023), and most recent listed session date (19 June 2026) were confirmed directly against MOF's own page, but membership and interim-report substance are open items.

This card treats the Bank of Japan's 9 October 2020 'no plan to issue CBDC' statement as BOJ's foundational, still-current published position because it remains linked from BOJ's live CBDC portal, but did not independently fetch a 2025-2026-dated BOJ primary source explicitly reaffirming 'no decision has been made' in those exact terms this session; secondary press reporting (not independently verified here) consistently describes BOJ officials repeating a similar position into 2026, and separately reports that a government/BOJ decision on digital-yen issuance is expected sometime in 2026 -- this card makes no prediction about that outcome or timing.

The 3 April 2026 FSA Payment Innovation Project tokenized-deposit interbank-settlement pilot names three participating firms in FSA's own announcement; consistent with this project's named-entity rule, this card describes the pilot's regulatory scope and purpose only and does not name the participants.

Stablecoin issuer eligibility (banks, registered fund-transfer service providers, and trust companies as permitted issuers of electronic payment instruments) and the 2025 PSA amendment's reserve-asset relaxation for trust-type stablecoins are tracked in full in the Stablecoins pillar; this pillar cross-references rather than duplicates that research. The pending 2026 FIEA/PSA migration bill's implications, if any, for banks' crypto-asset activity are tracked in the Exchanges (VATP) and Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillars; this pillar found no FSA statement specifically addressing that bill's effect on bank prudential treatment and makes no claim about it.
